content.text: Two Kinds of Ownership, Two Different Systems

Teams that manage Claude applications frequently conflate two distinct ownership concepts: who reviews a change before it is merged, and who is responsible for fixing it when it breaks in production. Both are necessary. Neither substitutes for the other. Conflating them creates gaps: prompt changes approved by the right reviewers but landing in an on-call rotation that has no context on AI behaviour, or production incidents paged to a team that owns the code but not the AI output quality.

Review ownership (CODEOWNERS) is a pre-merge gate: it controls who must approve a change before it reaches main. On-call ownership is a post-deploy responsibility: it controls who gets paged when a deployed change causes a problem. For conventional software, these often coincide. For Claude services, they often should not — the AI safety team reviews prompt changes, but they are not the right people to be paged at 2 AM about a latency spike in the triage service.

This sub-subtopic defines the alignment between CODEOWNERS and on-call ownership for Claude services: what belongs in each system, how to structure the OWNERS.yaml file that documents on-call ownership alongside the code, how to validate the alignment between the two, and how to handle the special case of AI safety incidents that require escalation to the @ai-safety-team regardless of which service is on-call.
Review Ownership vs. On-Call Ownership

attrs.alt: Review ownership (CODEOWNERS, @teams) vs. on-call ownership (PagerDuty, individuals) — two separate systems

content.text: Dimension
Review Ownership (CODEOWNERS)
On-Call Ownership
What it controls
Who must approve a PR before it merges
Who gets paged when the service fails in production
Who is listed
@team slugs (GitHub teams)
Individual engineers on the rotation
Managed in
.github/CODEOWNERS (version-controlled)
PagerDuty / OpsGenie / OWNERS.yaml
Changes when
Team boundaries change; new file types need review
Rotation schedules change; new SLOs defined
Consequence of getting it wrong
Wrong team reviews the change; review gate is ineffective
Wrong person gets paged; incident response is delayed
OWNERS.yaml — Documenting On-Call Ownership in the Repo

Keeping on-call ownership information inside the repository (alongside CODEOWNERS) creates a single location where both kinds of ownership are visible. The OWNERS.yaml convention (popularised by the Kubernetes project) documents on-call contacts, escalation paths, and service metadata:

# OWNERS.yaml — on-call ownership for the triage-service
# This file is for documentation and tooling only.
# It does NOT replace CODEOWNERS for pull request reviews.

service:
  name: triage-service
  description: "Claude-powered triage agent for customer support tickets"
  tier: 1    # tier 1 = highest criticality, 24/7 on-call

# On-call rotation — individuals, not teams.
# Updated when the rotation changes; do not list team names here.
on_call:
  primary_rotation:
    - alice@example.com
    - bob@example.com
    - carol@example.com
  escalation:
    # Escalation if primary is unreachable after 10 minutes.
    - dave@example.com       # engineering manager
  ai_safety_escalation:
    # Escalate to this person for any incident involving
    # prohibited model output or safety policy violation.
    # This person is from @ai-safety-team and is separate
    # from the service on-call rotation.
    - safety-lead@example.com

# PagerDuty service IDs for alert routing.
pagerduty:
  service_id: "P1ABC23"
  escalation_policy_id: "PE456XY"

# SLOs that trigger on-call pages.
slos:
  - name: "latency_p99"
    threshold_ms: 2000
    window: "5m"
    page_on_breach: true
  - name: "eval_pass_rate"
    threshold: 0.90
    window: "1h"
    page_on_breach: true
    # Breaching this SLO is a signal that model behaviour has degraded.
    # The on-call engineer must also notify ai_safety_escalation.
    notify_ai_safety: true

# Ownership of specific AI-layer components within this service.
# This mirrors CODEOWNERS but adds on-call context.
components:
  - path: "prompts/"
    review_owner: "@ai-safety-team"     # CODEOWNERS entry
    subject_matter_expert: "alice@example.com"  # who to call for prompt questions
  - path: "tools/"
    review_owner: "@api-team"
    subject_matter_expert: "bob@example.com"
  - path: "evals/"
    review_owner: "@eval-team"
    subject_matter_expert: "carol@example.com"

The `ai_safety_escalation` field and `notify_ai_safety` on the eval SLO are the critical entries that conventional OWNERS.yaml templates do not include. They create an explicit link between the service on-call rotation and the AI safety team for incidents that involve model behaviour.
Validation: Ensuring Alignment Between CODEOWNERS and OWNERS.yaml
The two ownership files can drift when teams change, when the on-call rotation is updated, or when new file types are added. A validation script checks that the two files are consistent:

# scripts/validate_ownership_alignment.py
"""
Validate that CODEOWNERS and OWNERS.yaml are consistent.

Checks performed:
1. Every path in OWNERS.yaml[components] has a corresponding CODEOWNERS rule.
2. The review_owner in OWNERS.yaml matches the CODEOWNERS rule for that path.
3. Every subject_matter_expert in OWNERS.yaml is in the on_call rotation
   or escalation chain (they should be reachable at incident time).
4. ai_safety_escalation is non-empty for any service that owns prompt files.

Usage:
    python scripts/validate_ownership_alignment.py

Exits with code 1 if any check fails.
"""
import re, sys
import yaml                        # pip install pyyaml
from pathlib import Path

REPO_ROOT = Path(__file__).parent.parent

def load_codeowners(path: Path) -> list[tuple[str, list[str]]]:
    """Parse CODEOWNERS into (pattern, owners) tuples."""
    rules = []
    for line in path.read_text().splitlines():
        line = line.strip()
        if not line or line.startswith("#"):
            continue
        parts = line.split()
        rules.append((parts[0], parts[1:]))
    return rules

def find_codeowners_owner(path_prefix: str, rules: list) -> list[str]:
    """
    Return the owners for the last CODEOWNERS rule matching path_prefix.
    Returns empty list if no match found.
    """
    matched_owners: list[str] = []
    for pattern, owners in rules:
        # Simple check: pattern starts with the same directory.
        if pattern.rstrip("/").rstrip("*").rstrip("*") == path_prefix.rstrip("/"):
            matched_owners = owners
        elif pattern == path_prefix or pattern.startswith(path_prefix):
            matched_owners = owners
    return matched_owners

def validate(owners_yaml_path: Path, codeowners_path: Path) -> list[str]:
    """
    Run all alignment checks. Returns a list of failure messages.
    """
    failures = []

    with owners_yaml_path.open() as f:
        owners_data = yaml.safe_load(f)

    codeowners_rules = load_codeowners(codeowners_path)

    components = owners_data.get("components", [])
    ai_safety_escalation = owners_data.get("on_call", {}).get("ai_safety_escalation", [])
    on_call_all = (
        owners_data.get("on_call", {}).get("primary_rotation", []) +
        owners_data.get("on_call", {}).get("escalation", []) +
        ai_safety_escalation
    )

    # Check 1: Every component path has a CODEOWNERS rule.
    for comp in components:
        path = comp["path"]
        review_owner = comp.get("review_owner", "")
        codeowners_owners = find_codeowners_owner(path, codeowners_rules)
        if not codeowners_owners:
            failures.append(f"OWNERS.yaml component '{path}' has no matching CODEOWNERS rule")
            continue

        # Check 2: The review_owner matches the CODEOWNERS entry.
        if review_owner and review_owner not in codeowners_owners:
            failures.append(
                f"OWNERS.yaml component '{path}' review_owner={review_owner!r} "
                f"does not match CODEOWNERS owners={codeowners_owners}"
            )

        # Check 3: subject_matter_expert is reachable.
        sme = comp.get("subject_matter_expert")
        if sme and sme not in on_call_all:
            failures.append(
                f"OWNERS.yaml component '{path}' subject_matter_expert={sme!r} "
                f"is not in the on-call rotation or escalation chain"
            )

    # Check 4: ai_safety_escalation is non-empty if prompts/ is owned.
    has_prompts = any(c["path"].startswith("prompts") for c in components)
    if has_prompts and not ai_safety_escalation:
        failures.append(
            "Service owns prompts/ but on_call.ai_safety_escalation is empty. "
            "Add an @ai-safety-team contact for model behaviour incidents."
        )

    return failures

if __name__ == "__main__":
    owners_yaml = REPO_ROOT / "OWNERS.yaml"
    codeowners = REPO_ROOT / ".github" / "CODEOWNERS"

    if not owners_yaml.exists():
        print("ERROR: OWNERS.yaml not found", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(1)
    if not codeowners.exists():
        print("ERROR: .github/CODEOWNERS not found", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(1)

    failures = validate(owners_yaml, codeowners)
    if failures:
        for f in failures:
            print(f"FAIL: {f}")
        sys.exit(1)
    else:
        print("OK: CODEOWNERS and OWNERS.yaml are consistent")

# .github/workflows/ownership-alignment.yml
name: Ownership alignment check

on:
  pull_request:
    paths:
      - '.github/CODEOWNERS'
      - 'OWNERS.yaml'

jobs:
  validate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.12'
      - run: pip install pyyaml
      - name: Validate ownership alignment
        run: python scripts/validate_ownership_alignment.py
AI Safety Escalation During Incidents

The standard incident response flow for a Claude service diverges from a conventional service at one point: when the incident involves model behaviour (prohibited output, unexpected refusals, format breakage caused by a prompt change), the on-call engineer may not have the context to investigate or fix the issue. The ai_safety_escalation contact in OWNERS.yaml provides a named escalation path.
The escalation criteria should be defined in the runbook, not improvised at incident time:

# triage-service Runbook — AI Safety Escalation Criteria

**Escalate to ai_safety_escalation when ANY of the following is true:**

1. The eval_pass_rate SLO has breached — a drop in pass rate indicates
   model behaviour has changed, potentially due to a prompt or model version
   change that slipped through the review gate.

2. Users are reporting outputs that appear to violate the behaviour
   specification (prohibited content, incorrect refusals, wrong format).

3. A `prompt(*) ` commit was deployed in the last release and the incident
   timeline overlaps with that deployment.

4. The model is returning a different output format than expected (JSON vs.
   prose, schema field missing) — this indicates a breaking prompt change
   may have bypassed CODEOWNERS review.

**To escalate:**
1. Page the ai_safety_escalation contact via PagerDuty runbook action.
2. Share the audit log query: the prompt hash currently in production
   and the eval that certified it.
3. Do NOT rollback the prompt independently. Work with @ai-safety-team
   to determine whether a rollback is the right action.
Testing Ownership Alignment

# tests/test_ownership_alignment.py
"""Tests for the CODEOWNERS / OWNERS.yaml alignment validator."""
import unittest, textwrap
from io import StringIO
from pathlib import Path
from unittest.mock import patch, mock_open
from scripts.validate_ownership_alignment import load_codeowners, validate

SAMPLE_CODEOWNERS = textwrap.dedent("""
    *              @service-team
    prompts/       @ai-safety-team
    prompts/**     @ai-safety-team
    tools/         @api-team
    evals/         @eval-team
""")

SAMPLE_OWNERS_YAML = {
    "service": {"name": "triage-service", "tier": 1},
    "on_call": {
        "primary_rotation": ["alice@example.com", "bob@example.com"],
        "escalation": ["dave@example.com"],
        "ai_safety_escalation": ["safety-lead@example.com"],
    },
    "components": [
        {
            "path": "prompts/",
            "review_owner": "@ai-safety-team",
            "subject_matter_expert": "alice@example.com",
        },
        {
            "path": "tools/",
            "review_owner": "@api-team",
            "subject_matter_expert": "bob@example.com",
        },
    ],
}

class TestLoadCodeowners(unittest.TestCase):

    def test_parses_rules(self):
        rules = load_codeowners(
            # Write to a temp file and parse it
            self._write_temp(SAMPLE_CODEOWNERS)
        )
        # Check that prompts/ rule was parsed
        patterns = [r[0] for r in rules]
        self.assertIn("prompts/", patterns)

    def _write_temp(self, content: str) -> Path:
        import tempfile
        tmp = tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(mode="w", suffix=".txt", delete=False)
        tmp.write(content)
        tmp.close()
        return Path(tmp.name)

class TestValidateAlignment(unittest.TestCase):

    def _run_validate(self, owners_yaml: dict, codeowners_text: str) -> list[str]:
        """Helper that patches file reads with in-memory data."""
        import tempfile, yaml
        # Write OWNERS.yaml
        oy_file = tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(mode="w", suffix=".yaml", delete=False)
        yaml.dump(owners_yaml, oy_file)
        oy_file.close()
        # Write CODEOWNERS
        co_file = tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(mode="w", suffix=".txt", delete=False)
        co_file.write(codeowners_text)
        co_file.close()
        return validate(Path(oy_file.name), Path(co_file.name))

    def test_consistent_files_pass(self):
        failures = self._run_validate(SAMPLE_OWNERS_YAML, SAMPLE_CODEOWNERS)
        self.assertEqual(failures, [])

    def test_missing_ai_safety_escalation_fails(self):
        import copy
        bad = copy.deepcopy(SAMPLE_OWNERS_YAML)
        bad["on_call"]["ai_safety_escalation"] = []
        failures = self._run_validate(bad, SAMPLE_CODEOWNERS)
        self.assertTrue(any("ai_safety_escalation" in f for f in failures))

    def test_sme_not_in_oncall_fails(self):
        import copy
        bad = copy.deepcopy(SAMPLE_OWNERS_YAML)
        bad["components"][0]["subject_matter_expert"] = "unknown@example.com"
        failures = self._run_validate(bad, SAMPLE_CODEOWNERS)
        self.assertTrue(any("subject_matter_expert" in f for f in failures))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    unittest.main()
Summary

On-call ownership and review ownership answer different questions and belong in different systems: CODEOWNERS defines which @teams must approve a change before it merges; OWNERS.yaml documents which individuals are paged when the service fails, and who to escalate to for model behaviour incidents. The two files should be consistent — every path listed in OWNERS.yaml components should have a corresponding CODEOWNERS rule with the same review_owner. The ai_safety_escalation field in OWNERS.yaml is the critical link between service operations and AI safety: it names the person to contact when an incident involves model behaviour, eval pass rate degradation, or a suspected prompt regression, giving on-call engineers a clear path to domain expertise at 2 AM.
